reasoning: To resolve YES, the number of ACLED 'Battles' in a 30-day period needs to exceed 10 times the baseline value. The baseline calculation is 1 + (average past-360 days 30-day total). Today, the baseline is 1.917, meaning there were roughly 11 battles over the entire past year (~0.9 battles per month) in Chile. The threshold to trigger a YES is > 19.17 Battles in 30 days — requiring at least 20 battles in a single month. This would represent an unprecedented explosion of armed conflict in Chile, far beyond the typical Mapuche-related violence, which is usually coded as riots or remote violence, not 'Battles' (which require two armed groups exchanging fire). Even if the baseline drops to precisely 1.0 in the future, it would still require >10 battles in a month. While gang violence (e.g. Tren de Aragua) is rising, it rarely involves two armed groups openly clashing in sustained battles multiple times a week. Given the complete lack of a 20-battle-a-month insurgency currently building up in Chile, probabilities are extremely low. I scale probabilities slightly upward for distant resolution dates to account for unpredictable long-term tail risks (like a catastrophic cartel war breaking out next year).
